title: Curriculum Attribute-Guided Hard Negative Mining for Fine-Grained Supervised Contrastive Learning
description: We propose CAG-HNM, a novel supervised contrastive learning method that combines curriculum learning with attribute-guided hard negative mining. Unlike fixed attribute-guided approaches like JD-CCL, our method progressively increases emphasis on hard negatives as training advances using a curriculum progress parameter. This provides both the stability of pre-computed semantic attributes and the training benefits of curriculum learning, with improved convergence and performance on fine-grained recognition tasks.
motivation: Standard supervised contrastive learning treats all negatives equally, while recent work (JD-CCL, NAACL 2025) proposed fixed attribute-guided selection using Jaccard similarity. However, fixed hard negative emphasis from the start of training can cause convergence issues. Curriculum learning suggests that learning from easy to hard improves performance, but existing curricular contrastive learning uses feature-based hardness which is unstable. We combine curriculum learning with attribute-guided mining—using pre-computed semantic attributes with progressive difficulty adjustment.
proposed_approach: Our method computes Jaccard attribute similarity between classes and weights negative samples by w_n(t) = exp(λ(t) · s_attr(y_i, y_n)), where λ(t) = λ_min + (λ_max - λ_min) · t^γ is a curriculum progress parameter that increases with training progress t. Early in training, all negatives are weighted similarly; as training progresses, attribute-similar hard negatives receive progressively higher weights. This combines stable attribute guidance with curriculum-based progression.

related_work.paper: Supervised Contrastive Learning (Khosla et al., NeurIPS 2020)
related_work.relation: Our base framework. We extend with curriculum-based attribute-guided weighting.

related_work.paper: JD-CCL: Enhancing Multimodal Entity Linking with Jaccard Distance-based Conditional Contrastive Learning (Nguyen et al., NAACL 2025)
related_work.relation: Closest prior work using Jaccard similarity for hard negative selection. We differentiate through curriculum-based progressive weighting vs. their fixed top-k selection, and focus on computer vision rather than multimodal entity linking.

related_work.paper: Mining negative samples on contrastive learning via curricular weighting strategy (Zhuang et al., Information Sciences 2024)
related_work.relation: Uses feature-based curriculum for contrastive learning. We use attribute-based curriculum which is more stable and interpretable.

related_work.paper: AMCL: supervised contrastive learning with hard sample mining for multi-functional therapeutic peptide prediction (BMC Biology 2025)
related_work.relation: Uses Jaccard similarity for hard sample mining in multi-label peptide prediction. We focus on single-label fine-grained recognition with curriculum progression.

related_work.paper: Supervised Contrastive Learning with Hard Negative Samples (Jiang et al., arXiv 2022)
related_work.relation: Uses feature-space similarity for hard negative mining. Our attribute-space approach is more stable.

related_work.paper: Multi-Similarity Contrastive Learning (Mu et al., arXiv 2023)
related_work.relation: Learns uncertainty-based weights across multiple projection heads. Our approach uses deterministic curriculum progression with zero learned parameters.

differentiation.vs_jd_ccl: JD-CCL uses FIXED top-k selection based on Jaccard similarity for multimodal entity linking. CAG-HNM uses PROGRESSIVE curriculum-based weighting for fine-grained recognition. Key differences: (1) curriculum vs. fixed, (2) vision vs. NLP, (3) weighting vs. selection.
differentiation.vs_feature_based_curriculum: Zhuang et al. (2024) uses feature-space similarity for curriculum. CAG-HNM uses attribute-space curriculum which is more stable and doesn't require online computation.
differentiation.vs_multi_similarity: MSCon learns uncertainty-based weights. CAG-HNM uses deterministic curriculum with no learned parameters.
differentiation.vs_amcl: AMCL uses Jaccard for multi-label peptide prediction. CAG-HNM focuses on single-label CV with curriculum progression.

hypothesis: Combining curriculum learning with attribute-guided hard negative mining produces more discriminative representations than both standard contrastive learning and fixed attribute-guided approaches (including JD-CCL), with improved training stability and faster convergence on fine-grained recognition tasks.

confirmed: CAG-HNM achieves statistically significant improvements (p < 0.05) over SupCon on at least 3 of 4 benchmarks
CAG-HNM outperforms JD-CCL-style fixed selection by at least 1.5% on fine-grained datasets
CAG-HNM outperforms or matches Curricular-CL (feature-based curriculum)
Ablation shows curriculum progression (γ > 0) performs better than fixed weighting
Method demonstrates improved training stability (lower variance across runs)
Convergence requires fewer epochs than feature-based curriculum approaches
refuted: CAG-HNM performs worse than or equivalent to fixed attribute-guided approaches
The computational overhead outweighs the accuracy gains
The curriculum schedule is unstable across different datasets

key_innovation: We are the first to combine curriculum learning with attribute-guided hard negative mining. This addresses the key limitation of JD-CCL (fixed difficulty) while providing more stable training than feature-based curriculum approaches. The curriculum progression is deterministic and requires no learned parameters.
expected_contributions: Novel combination of curriculum learning with attribute-guided hard negative mining
Progressive hardness adjustment via curriculum schedule λ(t) = λ_min + (λ_max - λ_min) · t^γ
Clear differentiation from JD-CCL through curriculum-based vs. fixed selection
Empirical demonstration that attribute-based curriculum outperforms feature-based curriculum
State-of-the-art results on fine-grained recognition benchmarks
datasets: CIFAR-100 (100 classes, derived attributes from 20 coarse labels)
Stanford Cars (196 classes, color + type attributes)

computational_requirements.training_time: ~4-6 hours for full experiments on all datasets
computational_requirements.memory_overhead: <1% additional memory for pre-computed attribute similarity matrix
computational_requirements.time_overhead: <3% additional training time (curriculum parameter update per epoch)
computational_requirements.parameters: Zero additional learnable parameters; 3 hyperparameters for curriculum schedule
